---
title: "Similarity: what makes two images aligned"
---

This chapter owns the *data term* - the number that scores how well a candidate transform aligns two
images, the $\mathcal{S}(I_f,\,I_m\circ\varphi)$ in the
[registration objective](foundations.qmd#sec-problem). The single idea it adds is that "aligned" is not
one thing. What counts as agreement depends on whether the two images are the *same modality* (compare
intensities directly - sum of squared differences) or *different modalities* (intensities are related by
an unknown, possibly non-monotone map - compare *statistical dependence* instead, via mutual information
or the correlation ratio). The cleanest way to organise the four metrics is by the *group of intensity
transformations each one ignores*: a metric is the right choice when the nuisance relationship between
the two intensity scales lies inside the group it is invariant to. This is the data-term face of the
unit's symmetry thread - where [foundations](foundations.qmd#sec-ladder) chose the *transform* rung by
the geometry's nuisance group, here we choose the *metric* by the intensities' nuisance group.

`nitrix` ships four metrics as an *algebraic data type* - `SSD`, `LNCC`, `MI`, `CorrelationRatio` - each
a frozen record carrying its own knobs and two capability flags (`is_least_squares`, `is_spatial_mean`)
that *dispatch the optimiser* for you. We climb from the metric you would invent first (least squares)
to the cross-modal workhorse (mutual information), earning each rung from the way the previous one
breaks, and end at the front-end that conditions the images before any metric sees them
(`preprocess_images`).

You have read the [registration problem](foundations.qmd#sec-problem): we look for a transform $\varphi$
that minimises a mismatch energy $\mathcal{S}(I_f,\,I_m\circ\varphi) + \lambda\,\mathcal{R}(\varphi)$,
*similarity* plus *regularisation* over a parametrised family. The
[transforms](transforms.qmd#sec-homogeneous) chapter built the family and the
[resampling](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling) chapter built the warped image $I_m\circ\varphi$ (the
moving image pulled back onto the fixed grid). This chapter fills in the one ingredient we waved past:
*$\mathcal{S}$ itself* - the data term, the metric. It is the part of the objective that decides *what
alignment means*, and getting it wrong is the single most common way a registration fails silently: the
optimiser converges happily to the minimum of the wrong score.

The source is [`register/_metric.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_metric.py)
(the metric records and the optimiser-facing flags) and
[`register/_preprocess.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_preprocess.py)
(the intensity front-end); the kernels they wrap live in
[`metrics/intensity.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/metrics/intensity.py)
and [`metrics/information.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/metrics/information.py).

## A motivating example: T1↔T1 versus T1↔EPI {#sec-motivation}

Hold two concrete tasks side by side for the whole chapter.

**Task A (same modality).** You acquired two T1-weighted scans of the same subject in one session and
want to realign the second to the first - a small head motion. Both images measure the *same physical
quantity* (T1 relaxation contrast), so a given tissue has (nearly) the *same intensity* in both. Grey
matter is mid-grey in both; white matter is bright in both. When the two are aligned, the intensities
agree voxel-for-voxel up to noise; when they are misaligned, a bright white-matter voxel sits over a
darker grey-matter voxel and the *difference* is large. So a sensible score of agreement is: add up the
squared intensity differences, and make it small. This is least squares, and it is the right instinct
here.

**Task B (cross modality).** Now you have a subject's EPI functional volume (or a T2-weighted scan) and
want to align it to their T1 anatomical. These measure *different* physical quantities. In T1, white
matter is *bright* and CSF is *dark*; in T2/EPI the contrast partly *inverts* - CSF is bright, white
matter darker. A tissue that is bright in one image is dark in the other, and the relationship between
the two intensity scales is *not* a simple gain-and-offset - it is an unknown, *non-monotone* function
of tissue type. Now the day-one instinct backfires: at perfect alignment the squared difference is
*large* (bright sits over dark), and an optimiser minimising squared differences will *mis*-register the
images to make bright land on bright - the wrong answer (@fig-similarity-crossmodal). The cure is a
different notion of agreement entirely: two images are aligned when knowing the intensity at a voxel in
one tells you the most it possibly can about the intensity in the other - when they are *statistically
dependent*, regardless of any intensity relationship. That is mutual information, and the correlation
ratio is its variance-based cousin.

The two tasks differ in *which intensity transformations should not change the verdict*. Task A wants a
metric that treats the identity intensity map as alignment; Task B wants one blind to an arbitrary
relabelling of intensities. The whole chapter is the elaboration of which score for which task, ordered
by how large a nuisance group each tolerates, and the design that lets a recipe carry the right one as a
single value.

## The metric as an algebraic data type {#sec-adt}

Before the metrics themselves, the shape they share - because that shape is what makes the optimiser on
the [next chapter](optimization.qmd#sec-objective) tractable.

The obvious way to let a recipe pick a metric is a string: `metric='ssd'`, dispatched by an `if`-chain.
That is what `nitrix` *used* to do, and it does not scale: every metric's knobs (the LNCC window radius,
the MI bin count) get flattened onto one god-config, the dispatch raises at trace time on a typo, and
adding a metric means touching the chain in several places
(`docs/feature-requests/registration-typing-metric-adt.md` records the migration). The house cure - the
same one the geometry unit uses for its `Interpolator` - is an *algebraic data type*: a `Metric`
*protocol* with one frozen `@dataclass` *implementer per metric*, each carrying its own
hyper-parameters, each exposing the same small interface. The driver then dispatches on a *method*, not
a string, and a new metric is one new record.

The `Metric` protocol (in `register/_metric.py`) is exactly four members:

- `is_least_squares` (`ClassVar[bool]`) - does this metric admit a *vector residual* $r$ whose
  half-sum-of-squares $\tfrac12\lVert r\rVert^2$ *is* the cost? Only `SSD` does. This single flag routes
  the driver to the *Gauss–Newton / Levenberg–Marquardt* least-squares path; everything else takes the
  scalar *BFGS* path. (We earn why least-squares is special in @sec-ssd.)
- `is_spatial_mean` (`ClassVar[bool]`) - is the scalar cost a *per-voxel spatial mean* (`SSD`, `LNCC`)
  rather than a *global histogram statistic* (`MI`, `CorrelationRatio`)? This flag is consumed by the
  diffeomorphic force machinery (`register._force.MetricForce`): the voxel-count rescale that makes an
  autodiff force match the closed-form sum-convention gradient is only meaningful for a spatial mean. (A
  histogram statistic has no per-voxel decomposition to rescale.)
- `cost(warped, fixed, *, mask=None)` → scalar - the *minimisation objective*, lower-is-better. Where a
  kernel returns a *similarity* (higher-is-better, like `lncc` or `mutual_information`), the record flips
  it: `1 - lncc`, `-mutual_information`, `1 - eta**2`.
- `residual(warped, fixed, *, mask=None)` → vector - the least-squares residual, for the
  `is_least_squares` members only; the others *raise* `NotImplementedError`.

Both `cost` and `residual` are **DIFF-JAX** - pure array math, jittable and differentiable, safe inside
the optimiser's `lax.scan`. The flags are `ClassVar`s (known at trace time, so the dispatch resolves
statically); reading them is **HOST-QA** in the sense that the *driver* branches on them on the host
before building the loop. The metric *math* itself does not live in these records - it stays in
`nitrix.metrics` (`ssd`, `lncc`, `mutual_information`, `correlation_ratio`); the records are a thin
*cost-orientation* wrapper. A consumer constructs a spec directly, e.g.
`RegistrationSpec(metric=LNCC(radius=2))`, and the driver reads `spec.metric.is_least_squares` to choose
its solver (`register/_core.py`). This is the same `NamedTuple`-of-data, dispatch-on-capability
discipline as the rest of the suite.

::: {.callout-note title="Why the flags, not the type, drive dispatch"}
The driver never asks *"is this an `SSD`?"* - it asks *"does this metric give me a residual?"*
(`is_least_squares`) and *"is its cost a spatial mean?"* (`is_spatial_mean`). Dispatching on *capability
flags* rather than the concrete class is what lets a future metric - a MIND descriptor, a self-similarity
context - slot in as one new record with the right two flags, and immediately get the right solver, with
no change to the driver. The flags *are* the contract.
:::

A second axis classifies the same four records, and it is the unifying thread of the chapter: each
metric is *invariant* to a group of intensity transformations - the nuisance relationships it refuses to
see. SSD is invariant to nothing (it reads the intensities literally); NCC to a global affine intensity
map $I\mapsto aI+b$; LNCC to a *spatially varying* affine map (a local gain and offset); the correlation
ratio to any deterministic *function* of the fixed intensity; mutual information to an *arbitrary*
reparametrisation of either image's intensities. Reading down that list is reading up a ladder of ever
larger invariance groups, and choosing a metric is choosing how much of the intensity relationship to
quotient away before scoring geometry. The rest of the chapter climbs that ladder.

## SSD: least squares, and why it is special {#sec-ssd}

Begin where Task A leads. Two same-modality images, aligned, agree intensity-for-intensity; misaligned,
they differ. Score the disagreement by the *sum of squared differences*. Per voxel the disagreement is
the residual

$$
r(x) \;=\; (I_m\circ\varphi)(x) \;-\; I_f(x),
$$ {#eq-ssd-residual}

the *warped-minus-fixed* intensity gap (note the order: `warped - fixed`, exactly as `SSD.residual`
computes `diff = warped - fixed`). The cost is the *mean* of its square,

$$
\mathcal{S}_{\text{SSD}}(I_f,\,I_m\circ\varphi)
  \;=\; \frac{1}{|\Omega_f|}\sum_{x\in\Omega_f} r(x)^2,
$$ {#eq-ssd}

which `SSD.cost` evaluates by calling `metrics.ssd(warped, fixed)` (the `reduction='mean'` default - the
ITK `MeanSquares` metric, verified bit-equal in fp64). Each symbol earns its place: $r(x)$ is the
residual at voxel $x$, the square $r(x)^2$ makes the cost insensitive to the *sign* of the gap and
smooth at zero, and the mean makes the cost independent of how many voxels are in the domain.

**When is SSD the right score?** Exactly when @eq-ssd-residual is a *meaningful* quantity - when the two
intensity scales are *comparable*, so that "the warped intensity equals the fixed intensity" is what
alignment *means*. In the invariance language above, SSD tolerates *no* intensity nuisance: it is the
right metric precisely when the nuisance group is trivial, the identity intensity map. That is the
same-modality case (T1↔T1, or a motion-corrected fMRI series, where every frame is the same EPI
contrast). It is the `3dvolreg` / Lucas–Kanade lineage. Cross-modality breaks the premise -
@eq-ssd-residual then measures a contrast difference, not a misalignment - the failure we will see in
@fig-similarity-crossmodal.

**The least-squares property is the special one.** Why does `SSD` alone carry `is_least_squares = True`?
Because its cost is *literally* the half-squared-norm of a residual *vector* $r = (r(x))_{x\in\Omega_f}$:
$\mathcal{S}_{\text{SSD}} \propto \tfrac12\lVert r\rVert^2$ (up to the $1/|\Omega_f|$ mean factor). That
structure is gold for optimisation. The gradient of a sum of squares is $J^\top r$ and its Hessian is
*approximately* $J^\top J$ - the *Gauss–Newton* approximation - where $J = \partial r/\partial\theta$ is
the residual Jacobian. $J^\top J$ is built from first derivatives only (no second derivatives of the
warp), is always positive semidefinite, and gives a step that converges quadratically near the optimum.
This is the engine the [optimisation chapter](optimization.qmd#sec-gauss-newton) runs for `SSD`, and
`SSD.residual` exists precisely to feed it the vector $r$ it needs. No other metric here is a sum of
squares, so none gives a Gauss–Newton Hessian for free; they take the general scalar
[BFGS path](optimization.qmd#sec-bfgs) instead. This is why the metric carries a `residual` method at
all: it is the hand-off to the fast least-squares solver. (`SSD.residual` returns the *raw* residual
`diff.ravel()`, mask-weighted by $\sqrt{\text{mask}}$ when masked - see @sec-masking for why the
square-root is exactly right.)

**Execution class.** `SSD` is **DIFF-JAX** throughout - the residual and cost are elementwise array
math, jittable and differentiable; the recipe differentiates through them, or uses
`linalg.implicit_least_squares` at convergence.

## LNCC: surviving a smooth intensity bias {#sec-lncc}

SSD assumes the two intensity scales are identical. The first crack in that assumption is gentle and
ubiquitous: a *bias field*. MR images carry a smooth, low-frequency multiplicative shading - the
receive-coil profile makes one side of the brain systematically brighter than the other. The *tissue
contrast* is intact everywhere, but $I_m(x) \approx b(x)\,I_f(x)$ for a smooth, spatially varying gain
$b(x)$. SSD reads the gain as misalignment and fights it (@fig-similarity-bias).

The fix is to compare *contrast* rather than *intensity* - to ask whether the two images *co-vary*,
which is insensitive to a constant gain and offset. That is *normalised cross-correlation* (Pearson's
$r$): subtract each image's mean, correlate, normalise by the standard deviations. By construction it is
exactly invariant to a global affine intensity map $I\mapsto aI+b$ - the first nontrivial rung of the
invariance ladder. `metrics.ncc` computes this *globally* (and is sign-agnostic as a cost via
`1 - ncc**2`, the ITK `Correlation` form). But a *global* NCC only removes one *global* gain and offset;
a bias field is *spatially varying*, so a single global normalisation cannot follow it. The cure is one
idea on top: make the correlation *local*, which widens the invariance from a global affine intensity
map to a *spatially varying* one.

`LNCC(radius)` is *local windowed normalised cross-correlation*. Around each voxel take a box window of
half-width `radius` (so $2r+1$ voxels per axis), and compute the *squared* normalised correlation
*within that window*. Writing $\tilde m,\tilde f$ for the window-mean-subtracted intensities, the
per-voxel local correlation is

$$
\mathrm{cc}(x) \;=\;
  \frac{\big(\sum_{w} \tilde m\,\tilde f\big)^2}
       {\big(\sum_{w} \tilde m^2\big)\big(\sum_{w} \tilde f^2\big)},
$$ {#eq-lncc}

the sum running over the window $w$ centred at $x$. The numerator is the squared local covariance; the
denominator is the product of local variances. $\mathrm{cc}\in[0,1]$; it is $1$ wherever the two images
are *locally* an affine function of one another - a local gain and offset are divided out, which is
exactly the bias field, *locally*. The cost is the complement of the spatial mean,
$1 - \overline{\mathrm{cc}}$, which `LNCC.cost` computes as
`1.0 - lncc(warped, fixed, radius=self.radius)`.

**The window sums are the whole computation - and they are cheap.** Equation @eq-lncc needs five
windowed sums per voxel: $\sum m$, $\sum f$, $\sum m^2$, $\sum f^2$, $\sum mf$. Each is a *uniform box
filter*, and a box filter is *separable* - a 3-D box sum is three 1-D box sums in sequence - so the cost
is independent of the window *area*. The implementation (`metrics._common._box_sum`) dispatches on
window size: a *shifted-slice sum* for the small radii LNCC actually uses (faster, and free of the
prefix-sum's fp32 cancellation), an *integral image* for large windows. The local covariance and
variances follow from the sums by the identity $\sum \tilde m\tilde f = \sum mf - (\sum m)(\sum f)/n$
(with $n$ the window count), the same algebra you would use to compute a variance in one pass. (This is
the [separable-filter substrate](../geometry/operators.qmd) shared with `geometry.spatial_gradient`.)

**Capability flags.** `LNCC` is `is_least_squares = False` - the squared, normalised, windowed ratio in
@eq-lncc is *not* a sum of squares of any residual, so there is no Gauss–Newton structure; the cost is a
single scalar and it takes the [BFGS path](optimization.qmd#sec-bfgs) (`LNCC.residual` raises
`NotImplementedError`). It is `is_spatial_mean = True` - the cost *is* a mean of the per-voxel local CC
over voxels, so the diffeomorphic force machinery can rescale it by the voxel count. Indeed LNCC is the
*diffeomorphic-recipe workhorse*, and it has a hand-written analytic force (`metrics.lncc_grad`, and the
ANTs centre-only `lncc_grad_center`) so the greedy SyN recipe never has to differentiate through the box
filters - see [diffeomorphic forces](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-forces). Its robustness to smooth
inhomogeneity is exactly why it, not SSD, drives that recipe.

**Default radius and the boundary.** `radius` defaults to `4` (a $9^3$ window in 3-D); smaller windows
track finer local contrast but are noisier. The box sums use a `reflect` boundary by default, which
keeps the window *count* uniform so the per-window normalisation is exact at the image edge (ANTs
instead trims to valid neighbourhoods; the interiors agree to fp64, only the boundary differs). When
masked, the mask gates the *reduction* - out-of-mask voxels do not count toward the mean - but, by
design, the windowed sums at an in-mask voxel still draw on its out-of-mask neighbours (a documented,
deliberate approximation; full window-gated sums would be a separate, costlier kernel).

**Execution class.** **DIFF-JAX**: the box sums and the ratio are all jittable, differentiable array
math.

@fig-similarity-bias shows the failure LNCC is built for. A smooth multiplicative bias field *depresses*
*global* NCC's peak - at perfect alignment the single global mean/variance normalisation cannot match a
spatially varying gain, so the score reports disagreement and the peak becomes shallow and broad (poorly
discriminating) - while the *local* windowed LNCC keeps a *sharp* peak at the true alignment, because
each window divides out its own local gain. (The point is the peak's *sharpness and placement*, not the
absolute level: squared local CC runs below $1$ on a textured phantom, so its scale is not comparable to
global NCC's.)

```{python}
#| label: fig-similarity-bias
#| fig-cap: "Why *local* normalisation beats global under a bias field. A same-modality pair, with the moving image corrupted by a smooth multiplicative gain $b(x)$ (left two panels: clean fixed, biased moving). **Right:** similarity vs a translation offset. Global NCC on the *clean* pair (grey) peaks at $1$ at offset $0$. On the *biased* pair (red) the smooth gain **depresses** that peak — at perfect alignment the global statistic now reports disagreement (the single global mean/variance normalisation cannot match a spatially varying gain), and the curve is broad, so the optimum is poorly discriminated. Local LNCC on the *biased* pair (blue) is far more **sharply peaked** at $0$: each box window divides out its own local gain, so the bias is removed *locally* and the score collapses steeply away from the true offset. (Squared local CC sits below $1$ on a textured phantom — its absolute level is not comparable to global NCC's; what matters is the sharp, correctly placed peak.) This locality is why LNCC, not SSD or global NCC, is the bias-robust / diffeomorphic-recipe default."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Same-modality phantom with structure spread across the field, so that a
# spatially varying gain genuinely corrupts a single GLOBAL normalisation.
N = 120
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:N, 0:N].astype(float)
base = np.full((N, N), 0.15)
blobs = np.random.default_rng(1)
for _ in range(9):
    bx, by = blobs.uniform(16, N - 16, 2)
    rad = blobs.uniform(7, 15)
    base = np.where((xx - bx) ** 2 + (yy - by) ** 2 < rad ** 2,
                    blobs.uniform(0.45, 1.0), base)
base = np.clip(base + 0.01 * rng.standard_normal((N, N)), 0, 1)
fixed = base.copy()

# A strong, spatially varying multiplicative gain: a coil-profile-like shading
# that brightens one side and dims the other.  The tissue contrast is intact.
bias = np.exp(1.3 * (xx / N - 0.5))             # ~0.52x left -> ~1.9x right
moving_biased = base * bias

def box_sum(x, rad):                            # separable box (reflect pad)
    k = 2 * rad + 1
    p = np.pad(x, rad, mode="reflect")
    c = np.cumsum(np.cumsum(p, axis=0), axis=1)
    c = np.pad(c, ((1, 0), (1, 0)), mode="constant")
    return c[k:, k:] - c[:-k, k:] - c[k:, :-k] + c[:-k, :-k]

def lncc(m, f, rad=4, eps=1e-5):                # mean local squared correlation
    n = (2 * rad + 1) ** 2
    sm, sf = box_sum(m, rad), box_sum(f, rad)
    smm, sff, smf = box_sum(m * m, rad), box_sum(f * f, rad), box_sum(m * f, rad)
    cross = smf - sm * sf / n
    vm, vf = smm - sm * sm / n, sff - sf * sf / n
    return float(((cross * cross) / (vm * vf + eps)).mean())

def ncc(m, f):                                  # global Pearson, in [-1, 1]
    md, fd = m - m.mean(), f - f.mean()
    return float((md * fd).sum() / (np.sqrt((md * md).sum() * (fd * fd).sum()) + 1e-12))

offsets = np.arange(-18, 19)
g_clean = np.array([ncc(np.roll(base, o, axis=1), fixed) for o in offsets])
g_bias = np.array([ncc(np.roll(moving_biased, o, axis=1), fixed) for o in offsets])
l_bias = np.array([lncc(np.roll(moving_biased, o, axis=1), fixed, rad=4) for o in offsets])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11.5, 3.8))
ax[0].imshow(fixed, cmap="gray", vmin=0, vmax=1); ax[0].set_title("fixed (clean)", fontsize=9)
ax[1].imshow(moving_biased, cmap="gray", vmin=0, vmax=1.6)
ax[1].set_title("moving × smooth bias field", fontsize=9)
for a in ax[:2]:
    a.set_xticks([]); a.set_yticks([])
ax[2].plot(offsets, g_clean, "-o", ms=3, color="0.6", label="global NCC (no bias)")
ax[2].plot(offsets, g_bias, "-s", ms=3, color="tab:red", label="global NCC (biased)")
ax[2].plot(offsets, l_bias, "-^", ms=3, color="tab:blue", label="LNCC r=4 (biased)")
ax[2].axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax[2].set_title("similarity vs offset under a bias field", fontsize=9)
ax[2].set_xlabel("translation offset (px)"); ax[2].set_ylabel("similarity (↑)")
ax[2].legend(fontsize=7.5)
fig.suptitle("A smooth multiplicative bias depresses global NCC's peak; local LNCC stays sharp at 0",
             fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.94])
plt.show()
```

## MI: the cross-modal workhorse {#sec-mi}

LNCC survives a *multiplicative* gain because a local correlation divides one out. But Task B is worse
than a gain: T1↔T2 contrast partly *inverts*, and the intensity relationship is *non-monotone* - there
is no gain, no offset, no even *function* in one direction that turns one modality into the other. A
correlation (local or global) measures *linear* covariation and goes to zero exactly where the
relationship is strong but non-linear. We need a score that does not assume *any* intensity relationship
- the top of the invariance ladder, invariant to an arbitrary reparametrisation of either image's
intensities - only that, at alignment, the two images are *statistically dependent*: knowing the
intensity here in one image tells you a lot about the intensity here in the other, whatever the unknown
correspondence between their scales.

That quantity is *mutual information*. Build it from the *joint intensity histogram* $P(i,j)$ - the
fraction of voxels whose moving intensity falls in bin $i$ *and* whose fixed intensity falls in bin $j$.
@fig-similarity-jointhist shows the key picture: at misalignment the joint histogram is a *smeared blob*
(a given fixed intensity co-occurs with many different moving intensities - little structure); at
alignment it *sharpens* into a few bright clusters (each tissue's fixed intensity co-occurs with one
characteristic moving intensity - even though that mapping is non-monotone). Mutual information is the
number that measures that sharpening:

$$
\mathrm{MI} \;=\; \sum_{i,j} P(i,j)\,
   \log\!\frac{P(i,j)}{P_m(i)\,P_f(j)},
$$ {#eq-mi}

where $P_m(i) = \sum_j P(i,j)$ and $P_f(j) = \sum_i P(i,j)$ are the *marginal* histograms of the moving
and fixed images. Read @eq-mi as a comparison: $P_m(i)P_f(j)$ is the joint histogram you would see if the
two images were *independent* (the outer product of the marginals); $P(i,j)$ is the one you actually
see. MI is the average log-ratio of the two - *how far the real joint is from independence*. It is zero
iff the images are independent (worst alignment) and grows as the joint concentrates (better alignment).
Equivalently, in entropies, $\mathrm{MI} = H(I_m) + H(I_f) - H(I_m, I_f)$: the marginal entropies minus
the joint entropy - alignment *shrinks* the joint entropy (the sharpening) while the marginals stay
fixed, so MI *rises*. The crucial property: MI depends *only on the statistical dependence*, not on any
intensity relationship - exactly why it is the *cross-modal workhorse*. Because relabelling either
image's intensities only permutes histogram bins, leaving the dependence structure intact, MI is
invariant to any such reparametrisation; it does not care that white is dark and dark is white, only
that the correspondence is *consistent*.

`MI(bins, normalized, range_moving, range_fixed)` (`MI.cost` returns `-mutual_information(...)`, the
negated similarity). The arguments:

- **`bins`** (default `32`) - histogram bins per axis. The histogram must be *differentiable* to sit
  inside a gradient-based registration, so it is not a hard count: each voxel contributes its *linear
  (order-1 B-spline / triangular Parzen) soft weight* to the four surrounding $(i,j)$ bin pairs
  (`metrics.joint_histogram` via `_soft_bin`). The bin *index* is piecewise-constant (zero gradient),
  but the gradient flows through the soft *weights*. This puts `nitrix` in the same B-spline-Parzen
  family as ITK's `MattesMutualInformation` (which uses cubic splines); coarser bins bias MI downward,
  and all soft/hard variants converge in the fine-bin limit.
- **`normalized`** (default `False`) - when `True`, return *Studholme's normalised MI*,
  $\mathrm{NMI} = (H_m + H_f)/H_{mf}$, in $[1,2]$. Plain MI can be inflated just by *more overlap* (a
  bigger shared field of view raises both joint and marginal counts); NMI divides that out, so it is
  robust to a changing overlap region as the transform moves - often the better choice when the field of
  view shifts during alignment.
- **`range_moving`, `range_fixed`** - the pinned $(\mathrm{lo}, \mathrm{hi})$ intensity ranges that set
  the histogram's bin edges. This is subtle and important enough to get its own paragraph.

**Range pinning: keep the bins stationary.** If you leave the ranges as `None`, the histogram uses each
image's *data min/max* to place its bin edges. That is convenient but quietly poisonous *inside an
optimisation loop*: as the moving image deforms across iterations, its min and max *drift*, so the bin
edges drift, so the objective you are descending *changes shape from step to step* - a non-stationary
objective, and the soft-bin assignment (hence the gradient) becomes piecewise-unstable (the design
record files this as A6). The cure is to *pin* the ranges *once*, before the
[coarse-to-fine loop](optimization.qmd#sec-coarse-fine), from the full-resolution images, and ride them
as a frozen constant. That is `pin_metric_ranges(metric, moving, fixed)`: for an `MI` (or
`CorrelationRatio`) with any `None` range, it resolves the range from the full-resolution data and
returns a `replace`-d copy with the range filled in. The resolution uses `_pin_range`, a
`lax.stop_gradient`-ed `jnp.min`/`jnp.max`:

- the *`stop_gradient`* keeps the bin edges *constant* - the histogram gradient is derived assuming the
  edges do not move, so a gradient must not flow back into them;
- using a *traced reduction* (not an eager `float(...)`) makes the pin *jit-safe* - under `jax.jit` the
  images are tracers and `float(tracer)` cannot run, but `jnp.min(tracer)` can. So `MI(bins=32)` traces
  with no explicit range even under `jit`, and the pinned value is unchanged.

This makes range-pinning a **HOST-CTOR**-flavoured step in spirit (resolved once, before the loop) that
is nonetheless trace-safe - the load-bearing rule from [execution classes](foundations.qmd#sec-classes):
pin the range *once* on entry, never re-derive it inside the jitted loop (precisely so `volreg` can
`vmap` the per-frame core). `pin_metric_ranges` is a no-op for a least-squares or already-pinned metric.

**Capability flags.** `MI` is `is_least_squares = False` (no residual; BFGS path) and
`is_spatial_mean = False` - its cost is a *global joint-histogram scalar*, *not* a per-voxel mean, so the
spatial-mean force rescale does not apply. (The diffeomorphic recipe still gets a fast MI force via the
closed-form Mattes gradient `metrics.mi_grad` / `nmi_grad`, not the spatial-mean machinery - see
[forces](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-forces).) **DIFF-JAX** throughout.

```{python}
#| label: fig-similarity-jointhist
#| fig-cap: "The cross-modal joint intensity histogram, the object mutual information scores. A two-class phantom is rendered in modality A (top-left, the fixed reference) and in modality B with **inverted** contrast (top-centre misaligned, top-right aligned). Bottom: the soft (Parzen) joint histogram $P(i,j)$ — moving bin vertical, fixed bin horizontal, on a log scale. At misalignment (bottom-centre) the scatter is **smeared**: a given fixed intensity co-occurs with many moving intensities. At alignment (bottom-right) it **sharpens** into a few bright cells: each tissue's fixed intensity maps to one characteristic moving intensity (even though that map is non-monotone). The joint entropy drops, so $\\mathrm{MI}=H_m+H_f-H_{mf}$ rises — MI reads the sharpening, not any intensity relationship."
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A two-class phantom rendered in two modalities (LUTs that invert contrast).
N = 120
yy, xx = np.mgrid[0:N, 0:N].astype(float)
cx, cy = N / 2, N / 2
r = np.sqrt((xx - cx) ** 2 + (yy - cy) ** 2)
label = np.zeros((N, N), dtype=int)
label[r < 46] = 1          # outer ring (GM-ish)
label[r < 24] = 2          # inner core (WM-ish)
lut_A = np.array([0.05, 0.55, 0.95])   # modality A
lut_B = np.array([0.10, 0.95, 0.35])   # modality B: NON-monotone vs A

def render(lut, shift=(0, 0)):
    sx, sy = shift
    lab = np.roll(np.roll(label, sy, axis=0), sx, axis=1)
    return np.clip(lut[lab] + 0.02 * rng.standard_normal((N, N)), 0, 1)

fixed = render(lut_A)
mov_mis = render(lut_B, shift=(14, 9))
mov_ali = render(lut_B, shift=(0, 0))

def joint_hist(m, f, bins=32):           # linear (Parzen) soft binning
    def soft(v):
        s = (v - v.min()) / max(v.max() - v.min(), 1e-12) * (bins - 1)
        s = np.clip(s, 0, bins - 1)
        lo = np.clip(np.floor(s).astype(int), 0, bins - 2)
        return lo, s - lo
    lm, tm = soft(m.ravel()); lf, tf = soft(f.ravel())
    H = np.zeros((bins, bins))
    for im, wm in ((lm, 1 - tm), (lm + 1, tm)):
        for jf, wf in ((lf, 1 - tf), (lf + 1, tf)):
            np.add.at(H, (im, jf), wm * wf)
    return H / H.sum()

def joint_entropy(H):
    p = H[H > 0]
    return float(-(p * np.log(p)).sum())

H_mis, H_ali = joint_hist(mov_mis, fixed), joint_hist(mov_ali, fixed)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(9.5, 6.4))
for a, img, t in ((ax[0, 0], fixed, "fixed $I_f$ (modality A)"),
                  (ax[0, 1], mov_mis, "moving $I_m\\circ\\varphi$ — misaligned"),
                  (ax[0, 2], mov_ali, "moving $I_m\\circ\\varphi$ — aligned")):
    a.imshow(img, cmap="gray", vmin=0, vmax=1); a.set_title(t, fontsize=9)
    a.set_xticks([]); a.set_yticks([])
for a, H, t in ((ax[1, 1], H_mis, "joint histogram — misaligned\n(smeared)"),
                (ax[1, 2], H_ali, "joint histogram — aligned\n(sharp clusters)")):
    a.imshow(np.log(H + 1e-6), cmap="magma", origin="lower", aspect="auto")
    a.set_title(t, fontsize=9)
    a.set_xlabel("fixed bin", fontsize=8); a.set_ylabel("moving bin", fontsize=8)
ax[1, 0].axis("off")
ax[1, 0].text(0.0, 0.6,
              "joint entropy $H(I_m,I_f)$\n"
              f"misaligned: {joint_entropy(H_mis):.3f}\n"
              f"aligned:    {joint_entropy(H_ali):.3f}\n\n"
              "alignment concentrates the\nscatter — joint entropy falls,\nso MI rises.",
              fontsize=9, va="center")
fig.suptitle("Cross-modal joint histogram: smeared at misalignment, sharp at alignment",
             fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.96])
plt.show()
```

## CorrelationRatio: functional, not merely linear dependence {#sec-cr}

Mutual information makes the *weakest* assumption - arbitrary statistical dependence - and pays for it
with a 2-D joint histogram and its bandwidth/bin sensitivity. There is a middle rung between "linear"
(NCC) and "arbitrary" (MI): assume the moving intensity is some *deterministic function* of the fixed
intensity - any function, not necessarily linear or monotone, but a *function* (one fixed intensity
predicts one moving intensity). In the invariance language, the correlation ratio is invariant to any
deterministic functional remapping of the fixed intensity, a group strictly between NCC's affine maps
and MI's arbitrary reparametrisations. That is the assumption behind Roche's $\eta^2$, the FSL
`mcflirt` default.

The idea is a one-way analysis of variance. Sort the voxels into *groups* by their *fixed* intensity
(the explanatory variable). If the moving image really is a function of the fixed, then within each
group the moving intensities should be nearly constant - all the moving variance should be *between*
groups, none *within*. So measure the fraction of the moving image's total variance that is *explained
by the group means*:

$$
\eta^2 \;=\;
  \frac{\sum_k n_k\,(\mu_k - \mu)^2}{\sum_x (I_m(x) - \mu)^2},
$$ {#eq-cr}

where $k$ indexes the fixed-intensity groups (bins), $n_k$ is the (soft) count in group $k$, $\mu_k$ is
the *mean moving intensity* in group $k$, and $\mu$ is the global mean moving intensity. The numerator is
the *between-group* variance (how much the group means spread); the denominator is the *total* variance.
$\eta^2 \in [0,1]$ is the *fraction of moving variance explained* - $1$ when the moving image is a
deterministic function of the fixed (perfectly grouped), $0$ when unrelated. Equivalently it is $1$ minus
the *unexplained* (within-group) variance fraction. The cost is its complement, $1 - \eta^2$
(`CorrelationRatio.cost` returns `1.0 - correlation_ratio(...)`).

`CorrelationRatio(bins, range_fixed)`:

- **`bins`** (default `32`) - the number of soft groups for the *explanatory (fixed)* image, using the
  *same* linear soft-binning as the joint histogram (so $\eta^2$ is differentiable; the per-group sums
  are soft scatter-adds).
- **`range_fixed`** - the pinned range for the fixed image's binning. Note there is *no `range_moving`*:
  CR bins *only* the fixed (explanatory) image, so it needs only the one range, and `pin_metric_ranges`
  fills just `range_fixed`.

CR is *asymmetric* by construction - the fixed image is the explanatory variable, the moving is
regressed on it. It captures *functional* dependence (more than NCC's linear, less than MI's arbitrary),
is cheaper than MI (a 1-D binning, not a 2-D histogram), and is the classic cross-modal cost in the FSL
lineage. Flags: `is_least_squares = False` (BFGS path; `residual` raises), `is_spatial_mean = False` (a
*global histogram statistic*, like MI). **DIFF-JAX**. (Note from source: there is no closed-form CR
diffeomorphic force - CR drives the diffeomorphic recipes only via the generic autodiff `MetricForce`
escape hatch.)

```{python}
#| label: fig-similarity-crossmodal
#| fig-cap: "Why SSD fails across modalities, in one sweep. A cross-modal pair (left bar bright in modality A, right bar bright in the inverted modality B) is scored as a horizontal translation offset sweeps; the true alignment is offset 0. **Left:** the intensity-difference costs — MSE and the sign-agnostic $1-\\mathrm{NCC}^2$ — put their **minimum at the wrong offset** (dashed red): SSD slides the moving until B's bright right bar lands on A's bright left bar, because that minimizes the squared difference even though the structures are then *mis*-aligned. **Right:** mutual information and the correlation ratio **peak at offset 0**, the true alignment — they score statistical dependence, which is maximal when the structures coincide regardless of the inverted contrast. (Curves min-max normalised for overlay.)"
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# Cross-modal pair: left bar bright in A, right bar bright in B (contrast inverted).
N = 100
def field(left_val, right_val, bg=0.10):
    img = np.full((N, N), bg, dtype=float)
    img[:, 20:35] = left_val
    img[:, 60:75] = right_val
    return img

fixed = field(0.90, 0.20)        # modality A: left bar bright
moving0 = field(0.20, 0.90)      # modality B: right bar bright (inverted)

def mse(m, f):
    return float(np.mean((m - f) ** 2))

def ncc(m, f):
    md, fd = m - m.mean(), f - f.mean()
    return float((md * fd).sum() / (np.sqrt((md * md).sum() * (fd * fd).sum()) + 1e-12))

def joint_hist(m, f, bins=32):
    def soft(v):
        s = (v - v.min()) / max(v.max() - v.min(), 1e-12) * (bins - 1)
        s = np.clip(s, 0, bins - 1)
        lo = np.clip(np.floor(s).astype(int), 0, bins - 2)
        return lo, s - lo
    lm, tm = soft(m.ravel()); lf, tf = soft(f.ravel())
    H = np.zeros((bins, bins))
    for im, wm in ((lm, 1 - tm), (lm + 1, tm)):
        for jf, wf in ((lf, 1 - tf), (lf + 1, tf)):
            np.add.at(H, (im, jf), wm * wf)
    return H / H.sum()

def mi(m, f, bins=32):
    H = joint_hist(m, f, bins)
    pm, pf = H.sum(1), H.sum(0)
    out = pm[:, None] * pf[None, :]
    nz = H > 0
    return float((H[nz] * np.log(H[nz] / (out[nz] + 1e-12))).sum())

def corr_ratio(m, f, bins=32):              # eta^2(moving | fixed)
    fv, mv = f.ravel(), m.ravel()
    s = (fv - fv.min()) / max(fv.max() - fv.min(), 1e-12) * (bins - 1)
    s = np.clip(s, 0, bins - 1)
    lo = np.clip(np.floor(s).astype(int), 0, bins - 2); frac = s - lo
    nk, sk = np.zeros(bins), np.zeros(bins)
    for idx, w in ((lo, 1 - frac), (lo + 1, frac)):
        np.add.at(nk, idx, w); np.add.at(sk, idx, w * mv)
    mu = mv.mean(); muk = sk / (nk + 1e-12)
    return float((nk * (muk - mu) ** 2).sum() / (((mv - mu) ** 2).sum() + 1e-12))

offsets = np.arange(-48, 49)
M, Nc, MIs, CR = [], [], [], []
for o in offsets:
    m = np.clip(np.roll(moving0, o, axis=1) + 0.015 * rng.standard_normal((N, N)), 0, 1)
    M.append(mse(m, fixed)); Nc.append(1 - ncc(m, fixed) ** 2)
    MIs.append(mi(m, fixed)); CR.append(corr_ratio(m, fixed))
M, Nc, MIs, CR = map(np.array, (M, Nc, MIs, CR))

def nrm(z):
    return (z - z.min()) / (np.ptp(z) + 1e-12)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4.2))
ax[0].plot(offsets, nrm(M), "-o", ms=3, label="MSE (↓)", color="tab:red")
ax[0].plot(offsets, nrm(Nc), "-s", ms=3, label="$1-\\mathrm{NCC}^2$ (↓)", color="tab:orange")
ax[0].axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.8, ls=":")
mse_argmin = offsets[int(np.argmin(M))]
ax[0].axvline(mse_argmin, color="tab:red", lw=0.8, ls="--")
ax[0].set_title(f"Intensity-difference costs\nMSE minimum at offset {mse_argmin} — WRONG", fontsize=9)
ax[0].set_xlabel("translation offset (px)"); ax[0].set_ylabel("normalised cost")
ax[0].legend(fontsize=8)
ax[1].plot(offsets, nrm(MIs), "-o", ms=3, label="MI (↑)", color="tab:blue")
ax[1].plot(offsets, nrm(CR), "-^", ms=3, label="CR $\\eta^2$ (↑)", color="tab:green")
ax[1].axvline(0, color="k", lw=0.8, ls=":")
ax[1].set_title("Statistical-dependence scores\nMI & CR peak at offset 0 — RIGHT", fontsize=9)
ax[1].set_xlabel("translation offset (px)"); ax[1].set_ylabel("normalised similarity")
ax[1].legend(fontsize=8)
fig.suptitle("Cross-modal translation sweep: SSD/NCC mislead; MI/CR find the true zero",
             fontsize=10)
fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0, 1, 0.95])
plt.show()
```

## Masking: scoring only the region you care about {#sec-masking}

Often you do not want the cost computed over the whole grid - you want it over the *brain*, not the
skull and the air around it, or over a structure of interest. Every metric `cost` (and `SSD.residual`)
takes an optional `mask`: a *non-negative per-voxel weight on the FIXED grid* (at the current pyramid
level's resolution). It restricts the cost to a region - but *how* it does so differs by metric kind, and
the difference is exactly the `is_spatial_mean` flag.

**Spatial-mean metrics (`SSD`, `LNCC`) weight by $\sqrt{\text{mask}}$ in the residual.** For SSD the
masked cost should be the masked sum of squares, $\sum_x \text{mask}(x)\,r(x)^2$. To keep the
*least-squares* structure intact, you cannot weight the *squared* residual - Gauss–Newton consumes the
residual *vector*, not its square. So `SSD.residual` weights the residual itself by $\sqrt{\text{mask}}$:
$\tilde r(x) = \sqrt{\text{mask}(x)}\,r(x)$, and then $\tfrac12\lVert\tilde r\rVert^2 =
\tfrac12\sum_x \text{mask}(x)\,r(x)^2$ is the masked sum of squares *automatically*. An out-of-mask voxel
($\text{mask}=0$) contributes a *zero row* to the residual, so it drops cleanly out of the
[Gauss–Newton normal equations](optimization.qmd#sec-gauss-newton) $J^\top J\,\delta = -J^\top r$ - it
neither pulls the step nor inflates the Hessian. The square-root is the unique weighting that makes the
masked *vector* norm equal the masked *scalar* sum of squares; that is why it, and not the mask itself,
is the right factor. (The `metrics.ssd` cost path applies the mask as a reduction weight,
$\sum(\text{mask}\cdot r^2)/\sum\text{mask}$; LNCC's mask gates the *reduction* of the local-CC map.)

**Histogram metrics (`MI`, `CorrelationRatio`) gate the scatter.** Here masking is not a reweighting of
a reduction - it is *exclusion from the distribution itself*. An out-of-mask voxel must not contribute to
the joint histogram $P(i,j)$ (MI) or to the per-group sums and the global variance ($\eta^2$, CR) at all
- otherwise the air and skull voxels would dump a huge, irrelevant cluster into the histogram and
dominate the statistic. So the mask *gates the scatter-add*: a masked-out voxel contributes nothing to
$P$, nothing to $n_k$/$\mu_k$, nothing to the total variance (`joint_histogram` and `correlation_ratio`
thread the mask into their soft scatter, the design's A3). This is the correct masking point for a
histogram statistic - gate the *evidence*, not the *reduction*. The `Metric.cost` docstring states this
contract directly.

In both cases the mask lives on the *fixed grid* because the cost is computed in fixed space (the warped
moving has already been pulled back onto it). The recipe threads the mask through `MetricObjective`
(`register/_objective.py`), and it is re-sampled to each pyramid level.

## Preprocessing: conditioning the images before the metric {#sec-preprocess}

There is a step *before* any metric sees the data: *intensity preprocessing*, the fMRIPrep /
`antsRegistration` front-end, in `register/_preprocess.py`. One honest note up front, exactly as the
contract demands: the `register.__init__` docstring's "Scope & validation" section lists winsorisation
and histogram matching as *"not yet implemented."* That note is stale - the code implements both.
`preprocess_images` exists and is wired into the recipes; trust the code over the docstring.

`preprocess_images(moving, fixed, *, winsorize_range, histogram_match)` applies up to two conditioning
steps *once* at the recipe's entry - so the conditioned images feed *both* the registration the transform
is estimated on *and* the returned `warped` output. Both arguments are *required keyword-only* (the
function has no defaults; the recipe layer supplies them, with its *off* values `winsorize_range=None`
and `histogram_match=False`, so an unconfigured recipe leaves the images byte-unchanged):

- **`winsorize_range`** - a `(lower, upper)` *percentile* pair (or `None` for no winsorisation). It calls
  `metrics.winsorize`, which clips each image to its `[lower, upper]` intensity percentiles (the fMRIPrep
  `--winsorize-image-intensities [0.005, 0.995]` convention): the bottom and top intensity *tails* are
  clamped to the percentile values, so a handful of extreme voxels - a hot artifact, a bright skull rim -
  cannot dominate an SSD residual or a Demons force. (It also relieves the velocity step-normalisation's
  global-outlier sensitivity: a clipped image has no extreme voxel to blow out the trust-region cap.)
  Pure and differentiable (a quantile plus a clip).
- **`histogram_match`** - a `bool`; when `True`, after winsorising it calls
  `metrics.match_histogram(moving, fixed)`, which *remaps the moving image's intensity distribution onto
  the fixed's by CDF / quantile transport* (the fMRIPrep `--use-histogram-matching 1` step). Concretely:
  estimate `bins=256` quantile knots of each image, then for each moving voxel read its position on the
  moving quantile curve and look up the fixed intensity at that *same* quantile (`jnp.interp` against the
  two quantile curves). The two images then share an intensity distribution, so a within-modality SSD /
  CC metric is not being pulled off by a global gain or offset. Differentiable, $O(N\log N)$ (the
  quantile sorts), paid once before the pyramid.

The order matters: *winsorise first, then match* (the matching transports the *winsorised* moving onto
the *winsorised* fixed). Histogram matching is a within-modality tool - it assumes the two distributions
*should* coincide, which is false across modalities, so pair it with SSD/LNCC, not MI/CR.

::: {.callout-important title="The estimate is conditioned; the transform is the deliverable"}
Preprocessing conditions the *estimate*, not your data. The real output is the estimated *transform*
(`result.matrix` / `result.displacement`); the returned `warped` is the *conditioned* moving resampled.
For the original intensities, apply the estimated transform to your *original* moving image - do not
consume the conditioned `warped` as if it were your data.
:::

## A different kind of similarity: boundary-based registration

Every metric in this chapter is defined on an *intensity grid* - a scalar field over voxels, scored
voxel-by-voxel or histogram-by-histogram. There is a wholly different way to score alignment, and it is
worth flagging because it does *not* fit the `Metric.cost(warped, fixed)` shape at all: *boundary-based
registration* (BBR, Greve–Fischl), the EPI↔T1 method of choice in the [recipes](recipes.qmd#sec-bbr).
BBR's cost is defined on a *surface* - the white-matter/grey-matter boundary, given as *points and
normals* - not on an image grid. For each boundary point it samples the moving intensity a short distance
to either side *along the surface normal* and scores the *cross-boundary contrast*
$(I_\text{out}-I_\text{in})/\tfrac12(I_\text{in}+I_\text{out})$, maximising it: a rigid pose that lands
the geometric boundary on a strong, consistently oriented intensity edge is the aligned one. Because its
signature is `cost(T, moving, surface)` - no `fixed` image - BBR is a *sibling objective*, not a
`Metric`: it composes the optimiser and the rigid [`TransformModel`](transforms.qmd#sec-fit) and a point
sampler through its own recipe (`register/_bbr.py`, the `BoundaryObjective`). It is the clearest
illustration that "similarity" is a family of contracts, of which the dense image-pair `Metric` here is
one constructor; the [optimisation chapter](optimization.qmd#sec-objective) unifies them under the
`Objective` protocol the driver actually minimises.

## Choosing a metric (a practitioner's summary)

The decision tree the whole chapter builds to - equivalently, a choice of how large an intensity
nuisance group to tolerate:

- **Same modality, comparable intensities** (T1↔T1, an fMRI series): *`SSD`* (tolerates no intensity
  nuisance). It alone is least-squares, so it gets the fast Gauss–Newton path. Add `histogram_match=True`
  if a global gain/offset remains.
- **Same modality with a smooth multiplicative bias field**: *`LNCC(radius)`* (tolerates a *spatially
  varying* affine intensity map). A local correlation divides out a spatially varying gain; it is also
  the diffeomorphic-recipe default and has a fast analytic force.
- **Different modalities, non-monotone contrast** (T1↔T2, EPI↔T1): *`MI`* (tolerates an arbitrary
  intensity reparametrisation; use `normalized=True` when the field of view shifts during alignment) -
  the cross-modal workhorse. *`CorrelationRatio`* is the cheaper middle rung when a *functional*
  (deterministic) relationship is a safe assumption (the FSL lineage).
- **Aligning EPI to its own T1 via the cortical surface**: not a `Metric` at all - use
  [BBR](recipes.qmd#sec-bbr).

Always *pin the histogram ranges* for `MI`/`CR` (`pin_metric_ranges`) before the loop, and *mask* to the
region of interest (the brain) so air and skull do not dominate the score.

## Further reading

- Viola, P., & Wells, W. M. (1997). *Alignment by maximization of mutual information.* International
  Journal of Computer Vision, 24(2). The paper that introduced MI as a registration metric - the
  cross-modal foundation of @sec-mi.
- Maes, F., Collignon, A., Vandermeulen, D., Marchal, G., & Suetens, P. (1997). *Multimodality image
  registration by maximization of mutual information.* IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging, 16(2). The
  companion derivation, with the joint histogram.
- Mattes, D., Haynor, D. R., Vesselle, H., Lewellen, T. K., & Eubank, W. (2003). *PET-CT image
  registration in the chest using free-form deformations.* IEEE Transactions on Medical Imaging, 22(1).
  The B-spline-Parzen soft histogram and the closed-form MI gradient `nitrix` follows (`metrics.mi_grad`).
- Studholme, C., Hill, D. L. G., & Hawkes, D. J. (1999). *An overlap invariant entropy measure of 3D
  medical image alignment.* Pattern Recognition, 32(1). Normalised MI - the `normalized=True` variant and
  why overlap invariance matters.
- Roche, A., Malandain, G., Pennec, X., & Ayache, N. (1998). *The correlation ratio as a new similarity
  measure for multimodal image registration.* MICCAI. The $\eta^2$ metric of @sec-cr (the FSL `mcflirt`
  cost).
- Avants, B. B., Epstein, C. L., Grossman, M., & Gee, J. C. (2008). *Symmetric diffeomorphic image
  registration with cross-correlation.* Medical Image Analysis, 12(1). The local (windowed)
  cross-correlation `LNCC` of @sec-lncc, as the SyN data term.
- Greve, D. N., & Fischl, B. (2009). *Accurate and robust brain image alignment using boundary-based
  registration.* NeuroImage, 48(1). BBR - the surface-defined sibling objective.
- Reuter, M., Rosas, H. D., & Fischl, B. (2010). *Highly accurate inverse consistent registration: A
  robust approach.* NeuroImage, 53(4). Robust intensity scaling / outlier handling, the motivation for
  the winsorisation front-end of @sec-preprocess.

## See also

- Source — the metric ADT, the capability flags, and range-pinning:
  [`src/nitrix/register/_metric.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_metric.py).
- Source — the intensity front-end (winsorize, histogram match):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_preprocess.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_preprocess.py).
- Source — the metric kernels (`ssd`, `ncc`, `lncc`, `lncc_grad`):
  [`src/nitrix/metrics/intensity.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/metrics/intensity.py).
- Source — the information-theoretic kernels (`joint_histogram`, `mutual_information`, `mi_grad`,
  `correlation_ratio`):
  [`src/nitrix/metrics/information.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/metrics/information.py).
- Source — the preprocess kernels (`winsorize`, `match_histogram`):
  [`src/nitrix/metrics/preprocess.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/metrics/preprocess.py).
- Source — the objective protocol and `MetricObjective` (how the cost reaches the driver):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_objective.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_objective.py).
- Source — the boundary-based sibling objective (BBR):
  [`src/nitrix/register/_bbr.py`](https://github.com/hypercoil/nitrix/blob/main/src/nitrix/register/_bbr.py).
- Previous in reading order: [resampling and the warped image](resampling.qmd#sec-pullback-sampling).
  Next: [optimization — how the metric is minimized](optimization.qmd#sec-objective) (the
  `is_least_squares` flag dispatches Gauss–Newton vs BFGS there). Then the cross-modal forces in
  [diffeomorphic registration](diffeomorphic.qmd#sec-forces) and the surface-defined
  [BBR recipe](recipes.qmd#sec-bbr). Sibling units:
  [geometry operators](../geometry/operators.qmd) (the shared separable-filter substrate),
  [statistics foundations](../statistics/foundations.qmd).
